Report From North Korea;Flood Waters Come and Go. Misery Endures.
nytimes_articles.abstract: North Korea's perpetual economic crisis may be turning into famine. Earlier this month, two United Nations agencies reported that "without emergency food imports the consequences are likely to be devastating for large segments of the  population." This week, Representative Bill Richardson, Democrat of New Mexico,  is scheduled to travel to North Korea, where few visitors are allowed. Trevor Page last week returned from six months there as country director for the World Food Program, one of the United Nations agencies. This article expresses his personal view of what he saw.

SOAPBOX;How I Spent My Summer Vacation
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IT'S Memorial Day weekend, the unofficial opening of the long, hot summer. New Jerseyans begin to look forward to long days on the beach -- or fishing, backpacking, biking or just relaxing. My colleagues here at Rutgers go to isolated cabins in the mountains of Vermont to write in splendid isolation, to Rome to photograph ruins and drink Campari, to East Africa to dig up old bones.  

     Me? I'm stuck in New Brunswick, teaching summer school. I am not alone. There are more than 800 summer instructors at Rutgers's main campus; some, like me, are regular faculty members, while others are graduate students who teach recitation sections in the humanities or lab sections in the sciences, and still others are part-time lecturers, most of them non-teachers with special expertise.

Life Among the Ruins
nytimes_articles.abstract: VALLEY OF THE SPIRITS A Journey Into the Lost Realm of the Aymara. By Alan L. Kolata. Illustrated. 288 pp. New York: John Wiley & Sons. $27.95.    HIGH on the wind-swept Bolivian plateau, at 13,000 feet, lie the ruins of Tiahuanaco. In 1978, a young archeologist, Alan L. Kolata, first set foot on the altiplano, beginning a long journey back into Tiahuanaco's past. Through 17 seasons of excavation and life among the ruins, he has become attached not only  to ancient Tiahuanaco but to its modern descendants, the Aymara, who inhabit the austere plateau surrounding the earth's highest lake, Titicaca. In "Valley of the Spirits," he weaves together the threads of history that tie the Aymara to the glorious past of Tiahuanaco.

VOICES: FROM THE DESK OF;Please Don't Fiddle While Money Is Burning
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE Philadelphia Orchestra is short of funds. In an effort to bring its finances under control, it will not fill the post of composer in residence and plans to reduce the amount it spends on guest conductors and soloists by 5 percent, according to an article this month in The Philadelphia Inquirer. 

     How typically old-fashioned and unimaginative, trimming marginal expenses, when the orchestra could use this opportunity to rethink the fundamental ways in which it works. Its leaders should show some 1990's vision and re-engineer the whole orchestra. Shake off all old assumptions, adopt the doctrine of Michael Hammer and James Champy ("Re-engineering the Corporation: A Manifesto for Business Revolution") and replace tired, ancient ways with redesigned processes  to achieve big improvements in critical contemporary measures of performance. Instead of a modest downsizing (doing a little less with a little less money), the orchestra might, through re-engineering, do much more with much less money.

270-Year-Old Dodge House Now a Museum
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHEN Marie Dodge Ross moved with her parents at age 3 to the family home in Port Washington to live with her grandmother, she became the seventh and last generation to occupy the home built by Thomas Dodge in 1721. The Dutch Colonial  farmhouse, perched over the Mill Pond and facing Manhasset Bay, is one of the oldest homes in the town of North Hempstead, and one of the few homes on Long Island inhabited for more than 270 years by the same family. 

     "The geneology kind of hangs over your head," said Mrs. Ross, who left home when she married and returned at age 70 to care for her parents. In 1993, after  moving to a nursing home, Mrs. Ross, who is in her 90's, sold the home to the Port Washington Water Pollution Control District.

NEIGBORHOOD REPORT: WILLIAMSBURG/GREENPOINT;Tools of the Trade: Art Sneaks Up on a Handyman's Haunt
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The hammers hang from Pegboard along one wall. The nails are in the back in a metal trundle. There are toolboxes and canisters of paint on the shelves. Everything in the fluorescent-lighted Crest Sentry Hardware on Metropolitan Avenue is as a carpenter's and electrician's supplier should be. 

     That is, until you look though a peephole in the closet door and see a figurine of a fetus in blue "amniotic fluid," or try to buy a switch plate made  from a sponge.

MAKING IT WORK;Someone to Watch Over Us
nytimes_articles.abstract:
LIKE sentinels frozen in time, some dozen forts still dot New York City's waterfront, their histories as complex as their futures are uncertain. 

     Since 1626, when the Dutch settlers built Fort Amsterdam at Bowling Green, nearly 100 armed batteries have been built. Forty-two were constructed during the Revolutionary years; some by the American forces (like Fort George, in Upper Manhattan), and some by the occupying British forces after General Washington retreated.

BOOKEND;The People's Publishers: China's Book Scene
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Our publishing house would like you to export a car for us from Boston to China," said my Chinese editor in Beijing in the summer of 1992. I pointed out that I was a writer, not a car salesman. "We want to build a long-term relationship with you," Wang Yaming bore on, an expectant look on his face. Hebei People's Publishers was cultivating an author in the spirit of China under Deng Xiaoping. 

      I declined the car deal, which would have saved huge taxes for the publisher and yielded me a 10,000 yuan commission ($1,854 at the time). Mr. Wang in turn declined my request for royalty payments for the Chinese edition of my "Mao," of which Hebei People's Publishers had sold a million copies.

SOAPBOX;Memories of a Lost Saloon
nytimes_articles.abstract:
ANOTHER small piece of New York's soul has died, the victim of an expired lease and a landlord's whim. The original Runyon's, a venerable East 50th Street saloon, has closed its doors for the last time. It might be trite to say that Runyon's was more than just another bar, but for anyone who ever passed through  its dark, narrow, smoky space, the closing, on May 3, was heartbreaking beyond belief. 

     Sure, there's another, more brightly lit Runyon's around the corner on Second Avenue. There's also Dodger Stadium instead of Ebbets Field.

A Foreign Affair
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WILDER TIMES The Life of Billy Wilder. By Kevin Lally. Illustrated. 496 pp. New York: Henry Holt & Company. $30. 

      BILLY WILDER, who will be 90 on June 22, started in the picture business in  Berlin at the end of the silent era. He once remarked that he was going to keep  at it until he got it right. His name on a movie is an assurance, or a threat, of irony, giddy sarcasm and lubricity - and that's just the comedies. Kevin Lally, the managing editor of the trade publication Film Journal International,  has now written "Wilder Times," the first full-length English-language biography of the director in a generation. If he hasn't caught Mr. Wilder's anarchic spirit, he does recount the facts of his life and examine each of his pictures.

Twilight Zone
nytimes_articles.abstract:
GREY AREA And Other Stories By Will Self. 287 pp. New York: Grove Press. $22.     WILL SELF is one of those unenviable writers who have become well-known for  all the wrong reasons. His name is weirdly eponymous. He takes very odd jacket photos. And, if his fictional characters are any indication, he knows a lot of different ways to get high. What none of this extraneous publicity blather prepareds anybody for, however, is the self-evident fact of Mr. Self's often incomparable talent. 

      The "Grey Area" of Mr. Self's latest collection of short stories describes an institutional landscape of reflective Formica tables, mindlessly humming fax  machines, chemcially sedated clinical depressives playing too much Ping-Pong and endless motorways without a decent service stop in sight. About the only act of  communication available in this world is the passing on of diseases, both mental and physical.

Festival Of Dancing And Light
nytimes_articles.abstract:
STEADY and rhythmic, the drumbeat rumbled across the lighted outdoor dance grounds into the darkness of the summer night. A gong sounded, flutes chirped, and several hundred women, men and children weaved and bobbed, arms sweeping to  the right, to the left and up toward the hidden moon. Our feet never stopped moving; periodically, we clapped in unison. 

     The dancers around me were a diverse group: elderly Japanese-American women wearing the cotton kimono called a yukata, their sons and daughters in happi coats, teen-agers in T-shirts and cut-off jeans, tourists in flowered shirts and shorts. Most dancers wore whatever seemed comfortable for a summer evening in Hawaii. Dancing feet were clad in Japanese geta, sneakers, sandals, slippers, espadrilles.

NEIGBORHOOD REPORT: BROOKLYN HEIGHTS;Once Grand St. George Hotel Eyed as Student Housing Site
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Empty rooms loom throughout the St. George Hotel, once the largest hotel in the city. Now it is largely vacant, home only to $50-a-night rooms, about 40 elderly couples, and a dim reputation. 

     But the 12-floor hotel, which is part of an eight-building complex at 100 Henry Street, may get an overhaul if negotiations between a not-for-profit housing group and the hotel's owners are settled. The St. George complex went from being a neighborhood nuisance to a real hazard after one of its buildings was destroyed by a fire last August.

VOICES: VIEWPOINT;Unlocking New Doors With Bonds
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE Treasury's adoption of "real," or inflation-indexed, bonds will provide an attractive alternative to conventional bonds for some investors, offering them precise inflation protection not currently available. The new bonds, the Treasury believes, will broaden the market for Government securities and eliminate the need to compensate investors for inflation risk. 

     In addition, the bonds now open the door for other governmental issuers.

Albania Democrats Rejoice
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Thousands of jubilant supporters of the governing Democratic Party took to the streets early today in the biggest celebration seen here since the fall of Communism six years ago. They were anticipating an official announcement that the pro-Western Democrats had held on to power in Sunday's election. 

      The Socialists, successors of the Communist Party, and eight other opposition parties announced midway through the voting that they were pulling out. They complained of harassment by the police.

How Easily We Killed Them
nytimes_articles.abstract:
We killed 170 people in Lebanon last month. Most were refugees. A good number were women, children and the elderly. Nine civilians, including a 2-year-old and a 100-year-old man, were killed at Sachmor, a village. Eleven civilians, including seven children, were killed at Nabatiyeh, a town. At the United Nations compound at Qana, a village, 102 were killed. We killed all these people not in a fit of inflamed passion, not because of messianic extremism or nationalist fervor. 

     We killed them under the umbrella of a peace campaign. Under the leadership of a peace Government and in the midst of an election campaign that features peace. We killed them so that peace could be re-elected. We killed them because  our peace coalition needed to prove it is just as tough as the opposition.

Raceway That Siphoned Off Aggression
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Xavier King, a self-described gang leader, used to spend hours under the hood of his souped-up Corvette preparing for the weekend races at the Brotherhood Raceway Park, where he would challenge everyone from rival gang members to law-enforcement officers. 

     "I could take the money I earn and put it into negative things, like guns for my homies," Mr. King said the other day. "Instead, I put it into my race car." Sometimes, he added, he would bring his gang members out to the park, a quarter-mile asphalt drag strip at the Port of Los Angeles, to keep them out of  trouble.

wiki_events.headline: First Chechen War: Russian president Boris Yeltsin meets with Chechen rebels for the first time and negotiates a cease-fire.

pages.title: First Chechen War
pages.description: 1994–1996 war between Russia and Chechen separatists
pages.abstract: The First Chechen War, also referred to as the First Russo-Chechen War, was a conflict between the separatist Chechen Republic of Ichkeria and the Russian Federation from 1994 to 1996. The conflict ended in a peace treaty that saw Russian forces withdraw from the territory only for them to invade again three years later sparking the Second Chechen War of 1999–2009.

pages.title: Boris Yeltsin
pages.abstract: Boris Nikolayevich Yeltsin was a Soviet and Russian politician who served as President of Russia from 1991 to 1999. He was a member of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union (CPSU) from 1961 to 1990. He later stood as a political independent, during which time he was viewed as being ideologically aligned with liberalism.

pages.title: Chechnya
pages.description: Republic of Russia in the North Caucasus
pages.abstract: Chechnya, officially the Chechen Republic, is a republic of Russia. It is situated in the North Caucasus of Eastern Europe, between the Caspian Sea and Black Sea. The republic forms a part of the North Caucasian Federal District, and shares land borders with Georgia to its south; with the Russian republics of Dagestan, Ingushetia, and North Ossetia–Alania to its east, north, and west; and with Stavropol Krai to its northwest.

wiki_events.headline: U.S. President Bill Clinton's former business partners in the Whitewater land deal, Jim McDougal and Susan McDougal, and the Governor of Arkansas, Jim Guy Tucker, are convicted of fraud.

pages.title: Bill Clinton
pages.description: President of the United States from 1993 to 2001
pages.abstract: William Jefferson Clinton is an American politician and lawyer who served as the 42nd president of the United States from 1993 to 2001. A member of the Democratic Party, he previously served as the attorney general of Arkansas from 1977 to 1979 and as the governor of Arkansas from 1979 to 1981, and again from 1983 to 1992. His centrist "Third Way" political philosophy became known as Clintonism, which dominated his presidency and the succeeding decades of Democratic Party history.

pages.title: Whitewater controversy
pages.abstract: The Whitewater controversy, Whitewater scandal, or simply Whitewater, was an American political controversy during the 1990s, surrounding the Whitewater Development Corporation, a real estate company owned by President Bill Clinton and First Lady Hillary Clinton and their associates, Jim and Susan McDougal.

pages.title: Jim McDougal
pages.abstract: James Bert McDougal and his wife, Susan McDougal, were financial partners with Bill Clinton and Hillary Clinton in the real estate venture that led to the Whitewater political scandal of the 1990s. Starting in 1982, McDougal operated Madison Guaranty Savings and Loan Association.

pages.title: Susan McDougal
pages.abstract: Susan Carol McDougal is an American real estate investor who served prison time as a result of being convicted of fraud in the Whitewater controversy.

pages.title: Governor of Arkansas
pages.abstract: The governor of Arkansas is the head of government of the U.S. state of Arkansas. The governor is the head of the executive branch of the Arkansas government and is charged with enforcing state laws.

pages.title: Jim Guy Tucker
pages.abstract: James Guy Tucker Jr. was an American politician, businessman and attorney who served as the 43rd governor of Arkansas from 1992 until his resignation in 1996 after his conviction for fraud during the Whitewater affair. A member of the Democratic Party, he previously served as the 15th lieutenant governor, state attorney general, and as a U.S. representative.
